name: Scottish Gaelic
notes: Scottish Gaelic (Gàidhlig). ~57,000 speakers (2011 census). Highlands and Islands of Scotland. PERVASIVE BROAD/SLENDER (leathann/caol) CONSONANT QUALITY: a consonant is slender (palatalised) beside ⟨e i⟩ and broad beside ⟨a o u⟩ ('caol le caol agus leathann le leathann'), and the flanking vowel letter that signals the quality is very often NOT itself pronounced — it is a quality marker, not a nucleus. Both halves are encoded in positional_graphemes: consonants take before_front_vowel/after_front_vowel slender variants, and the marker vowels map to the empty string. Stops are voiceless throughout (⟨b d g⟩ = unaspirated /p t̪ k/, ⟨p t c⟩ = aspirated), and slender velars are palatalised /kʲ kʲʰ/ rather than palatal /c ɟ/. Preaspiration of ⟨p t c⟩ after stressed vowels is a real, strongly dialectal feature and is deliberately NOT encoded (six distinct dialect patterns). Refs: Wikipedia 'Scottish Gaelic orthography'/'Scottish Gaelic phonology', Bauer (2011), Ó Maolalaigh (2008).

sources.notes: Reference grammar of Gaelic pronunciation; source for the broad/slender consonant series and the vowel-digraph values. Consulted via the Akerbeltz site; no printed page verified.

sources.author: Scottish Qualifications Authority
sources.title: Gaelic Orthographic Conventions (GOC)
sources.notes: The standard orthography, incl. 'caol le caol agus leathann le leathann' and the grave-accent-only convention.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Scottish Gaelic orthography
sources.notes: Accessible overview; source of the vowel-digraph table (⟨ai⟩ /a/, ⟨ea⟩ /e ɤ ɛ a/, ⟨ei⟩ /e/, ⟨io⟩, ⟨ui⟩ /ɯ/, ⟨eu⟩ /eː/, ⟨ao⟩ /ɯː/) and of the lenition values of ⟨bh mh ch dh gh th sh fh⟩.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Scottish Gaelic phonology
sources.notes: Accessible overview; source of the consonant inventory used here: /p pʰ t̪ t̪ʰ tʲ tʲʰ k kʰ kʲ kʲʰ/, /s̪ ʃ x ç ɣ ʝ/, /m n̪ˠ n ɲ/, /l̪ˠ l ʎ/, /r ɾ ɾʲ/ — i.e. palatalised /kʲ/ not palatal /c/, and dental /s̪/.

location.notes: Glottolog's representative point for Scottish Gaelic.

allophone_rules.notes: Preaspiration: Scottish Gaelic preaspirates the aspirated stops ⟨p t c⟩ medially and finally after a stressed vowel — the diagnostic feature that separates Gaelic from Irish (Wikipedia, 'Scottish Gaelic phonology' and 'Preaspiration'; Ó Maolalaigh 2008). The realisation is strongly dialectal (six attested patterns, [xk ht hp] ~ [ʰk ʰt ʰp] ~ none); the mainstream pattern encoded here is [xk]/[çkʲ] for the velars and [h]+stop for the coronals and labials. Conditioned on a preceding vowel, so word-initial and post-consonantal stops keep their plain aspirated realisation.

stress.notes: Scottish Gaelic stresses the FIRST syllable of the word (Wikipedia, 'Scottish Gaelic phonology'); unstressed vowels reduce to /ə/.
